one_line_takeaway: LLM agents can reproduce and in the sparsest regime exceed classical hand-engineered deanonymization attacks (79.2% vs 56.0% LSR on sparse Netflix Prize matching), and on the new INFERLINK benchmark they sometimes infer identities as an unintended byproduct of ostensibly benign tasks (Claude 4.5 LSR up to 0.80 under implicit framing) -- while an explicit anti-linkage system prompt can suppress this risk but induces a measurable, model-dependent utility cost (e.g., Claude 4.5 utility drop of 0.54 under explicit named-target framing).
key_finding: In the sparsest Netflix regime (m=2 overlapping movies), GPT-5 achieves LSR = 79.17% +/- 4.97 vs. 56.0%-60.2% for the classical hand-tuned baseline (T=14/21); GPT-5 approaches ceiling at m=4 (94.83% +/- 2.22), m=6 (97.43% +/- 3.76), and m=8 (99.00% +/- 0.72). Claude 4.5 is more sensitive to sparsity, dropping to 53.30% +/- 19.21 at m=2 but reaching 93.13%-97.30% for m>=4. AOL: the agent achieved Confirmed Linkage Count = 10 out of 40 selected histories via open-ended web retrieval and corroboration. INFERLINK: under IMPLICIT (benign) framing, Claude 4.5 shows substantial 'silent' linkage (LSR in [0.70, 0.80] across fingerprint types) while GPT-5 is more conservative (LSR in [0.00, 0.25]) at comparably high utility; under EXPLICIT-MK (named target), Claude 4.5 reaches LSR in [0.95, 1.00] across all fingerprint types, while GPT-5 is more robust specifically to COORDINATE cues (LSR=0.65) than to INTRINSIC/HYBRID. The privacy-aware system-prompt mitigation reduces EXPLICIT-MK LSR to near zero for GPT-5 and Claude 4.5 but at a cost: GPT-5 utility drops modestly (0.87->0.82 aggregated IMPLICIT-to-MK range per paper's before/after table), whereas Claude 4.5 shows substantial over-refusal (utility 0.99->0.45 under EXPLICIT-MK, a 0.54 drop) alongside its larger privacy gain (LSR 0.98->0.03 under EXPLICIT-MK example row for GPT-5, and Claude 4.5's own before/after MK row shows LSR 0.98->0.03 with utility 0.99->0.45). Human-AI trace studies: Anthropic Interviewer case yields CLC=6 (matching the count independently reported by concurrent work Li 2026, though case overlap is unknown); ChatGPT logs yield CLC=1 out of 30 analyzed privacy-relevant conversations, with the single success independently verified against a known internal group of <=12 candidates.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'inference-driven linkage': a privacy failure mode where an LLM-based agent reconstructs a specific real-world identity by combining scattered, individually non-identifying cues from an anonymized artifact with corroborating auxiliary/public evidence, without bespoke re-identification engineering. It introduces a shared evaluation interface Pi: (D_anon, D_aux) -> (identity hypothesis, supporting evidence), and applies it across three complementary settings: (1) reproductions of the classical Netflix Prize and AOL search-log deanonymization incidents using modern LLM agents, (2) INFERLINK, a new controlled benchmark (180 instances: 3 fingerprint types x 3 intent/knowledge conditions x 20 paired instances) that isolates how fingerprint type, task framing, and attacker knowledge affect linkage, and (3) case studies on redacted human-AI interaction traces (the Anthropic Interviewer dataset and anonymized ChatGPT logs). The authors also test a privacy-aware system-prompt mitigation and measure its utility cost.
threat_model: Attacker/agent is given (or can retrieve) an anonymized artifact D_anon (identifiers removed) and has access to auxiliary context D_aux (public web, social media, professional directories, provided auxiliary tables, or retrieved evidence) that overlaps with D_anon through shared quasi-identifying cues. The agent's objective is to produce an identity hypothesis i-hat plus supporting evidence E subset of D_anon union D_aux. Three settings instantiate this differently: in the Netflix case D_aux is a fixed noisy auxiliary trace and the agent matches it against a fixed 1,000-user candidate pool (no external web access, sandboxed OpenHands environment); in the AOL case and the human-AI interaction case studies, D_aux is not given but is built by the agent itself via open-web retrieval (web-enabled Gemini agent); in INFERLINK, D_anon and D_aux are two structured 10-record/9-feature tables presented incrementally in a multi-turn dialogue, with no external retrieval needed since ground truth is pre-specified. Task intent (implicit/benign vs. explicit re-identification) and attacker knowledge (zero-knowledge vs. named-target) are varied as independent conditions in INFERLINK.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: INFERLINK is intentionally simplified (single ground-truth overlap per instance, fixed schema), leaving harder regimes such as near-duplicate individuals to future work. The AOL and human-AI-trace case studies cannot establish base-rate prevalence of linkage in the wild because corroborable public records are scarce even for redacted traces -- they only show linkage occurs, not how often. The utility metric is deliberately scoped to task completion, not output quality, so it does not capture finer-grained utility degradation. The Netflix and AOL agent experiments use only two/one specific frontier models respectively (not the full three-model set used in INFERLINK), and the ChatGPT-log case has no reliable user-level ground truth (session-to-user mapping unknown), so CLC=1/30 is a lower-bound/conservative count rather than a rate. The authors explicitly disclaim broader claims: they do not argue all weak cues are dangerous or all cross-source reasoning is harmful.

datasets_used: Netflix Prize dataset (ratings, movie IDs, dates) - resampled 1,000-user pools x 3 resamples
AOL search log release (2006) - subset of ~1,612,860 histories with >200 queries and location signals, 40 selected
INFERLINK (newly constructed controlled benchmark: 180 instances = 3 fingerprint types x 3 intent/knowledge settings x 20 paired instances, each a 10-record x 9-feature paired table)
Anthropic Interviewer dataset (Handa et al. 2025), Scientists subset, redacted AI-use interviews
Anonymized ChatGPT conversation logs, 1,916 raw sessions -> 30 privacy-relevant conversations after LLM-judge filtering and PII masking
quantitative_results: AOL Confirmed Linkage Count: 10 of 40 selected histories
INFERLINK IMPLICIT LSR: Claude 4.5 in [0.70, 0.80]; GPT-5 in [0.00, 0.25] across INTRINSIC/COORDINATE/HYBRID fingerprints
INFERLINK EXPLICIT-MK LSR: Claude 4.5 in [0.95, 1.00]; GPT-5 = 0.65 (COORDINATE) vs higher for INTRINSIC/HYBRID
Mitigation (privacy-aware system prompt), EXPLICIT-MK, Claude 4.5: LSR 0.98(before, avg)->0.03(after); Utility 0.99->0.45 (drop of 0.54)
Mitigation, EXPLICIT-MK, GPT-5: LSR 0.85(before)->0.02(after); Utility 0.87->0.82 (drop of 0.05)
Anthropic Interviewer case: CLC = 6 (matches independent count from concurrent work Li 2026)
ChatGPT logs case: CLC = 1 of 30 analyzed conversations, independently verified against a known group of <=12 candidates
baselines_compared: Classical hand-engineered Netflix deanonymization baseline (Narayanan & Shmatikov 2008): rarity-weighted similarity with rating/date tolerances (T=14, T=21) and eccentricity thresholds

Cross-model comparison: o4-mini, GPT-5, Claude 4.5 (all run inside the OpenHands agent framework) as the 'agents' being evaluated, not baselines in the traditional sense but compared against each other for susceptibility
